1: The king and Haman attend Esther’s second banquet. The first banquet (Esther 5:4-8) had generated only a delayed petition; now the moment of revelation is at hand. Persian royal banquets followed a ritual sequence of courses, and the “banquet of wine” (mišᵉtēh hayyayin) marks the after-dinner phase when petitions were traditionally made. The twice-repeated offer (Esther 5:3, 6) is now repeated a third time in Esther 7:2 — the tripling intensifies the formal obligation to answer.
2: “What is your petition, Queen Esther? It shall be granted to you. And what is your request? Even up to half the kingdom, it shall be fulfilled.” The formulaic offer has been repeated three times; Esther’s strategy of delay — building suspense and royal commitment — has created the maximum possible obligation. The king’s repetition of the full formula signals that his patience is at its limit and Esther must now speak.
3: “Let my life be given me at my petition, and my people at my request.” Esther’s petition begins with herself (napšî...ʿammî) — her own life before her people’s — which makes the threat personal and immediate for the king. The phrasing shifts from the abstract “request” of her delay banquets to the concrete stakes of survival. The phrase ʾim-māṣāʾṯî ḥēn bᵉʿênê hammelek (if I have found favor in the king’s eyes) echoes the formula of covenantal appeal throughout the OT.
4: “For we have been sold (nimkarᵉnû) — I and my people — to be destroyed, to be killed, and to be annihilated.” Esther uses the exact triple verb formula (lᵉhašmîḏ lahᵃrōḡ ûlᵉʾabbēḏ) from the original decree (Esther 3:13), connecting her petition directly to Haman’s language. The word nimkarᵉnû (sold) implies that money changed hands — the ten thousand talents of silver Haman offered the king (Esther 3:9) — framing the Jews as commodities rather than citizens. “Had we only been sold as slaves, I would have held my tongue” — enslavement is tolerable; annihilation is not; the distinction defines the threshold of intercession.
5: “Who is he, and where is he, who has presumed in his heart (ʾᵃšer mᵉlāʾô libbô) to do this?” The idiom “whose heart is full to do” implies moral presumption — the fullness of heart that oversteps its authority. The king’s question is dramatic: he has signed the decree, accepted the silver, and yet asks who is responsible — either he has forgotten, or he has not connected the decree to its application to his own queen.
6: “The adversary and enemy (ʾîš ṣar wᵉʾôyēḇ) is this wicked Haman!” The double epithet — ṣar (adversary/oppressor) and ʾôyēḇ (enemy) — precisely mirrors the description of Israel’s covenant enemies in the Psalms and Lamentations. Haman’s terror before both the king and queen signals the complete collapse of his position; the man who minutes ago was being honored in the streets now stands accused before the throne that authorized his decree.
7: The king withdraws to the palace garden — a standard Persian royal practice of deliberation before judgment. Haman recognizes that the king has decided his fate (kî-kāltāh ʾēlāyw hārāʿāh min-hammelek — “evil was determined against him by the king”) and falls on the couch where Esther reclines to plead for his life. The act of a man falling on a woman’s couch in the royal inner chambers was a severe breach of protocol — access to the queen’s person was as restricted as access to the king.
8: The king returns to find Haman on Esther’s couch: “Will he even assault the queen (hᵃgam likḇôš ʾeṯ-hammalᵉkāh) in my own house?” The king misreads prostration as assault — whether deliberately or genuinely is unclear. The word likḇôš (to subdue/assault) is strong and legal. As the words leave the king’s mouth, attendants cover Haman’s face — the Persian signal of a condemned man, documented in Herodotus and Plutarch: once the royal face is averted and the prisoner’s face covered, the death sentence is irrevocable.
9: Harbonah, one of the king’s eunuchs, volunteers the information about the gallows: “There is also the gallows, fifty cubits high, that Haman made for Mordecai, who spoke up for the king” — reminding the king that Mordecai, the man just publicly honored, had been scheduled for execution by this same Haman. The eunuch’s intervention is providentially timed and politically acute; his epithet for Mordecai — “who spoke up for the king” — reactivates the chronicle narrative from chapter 6.
10: “So they hanged Haman on the gallows he had prepared for Mordecai, and the king’s wrath was appeased.” The irony is structural: the fifty-cubit height intended to maximize public shame for Mordecai now maximizes public shame for Haman. The verb wattiškaḵ ḥᵃmaṯ hammelek — “the king’s wrath subsided/was relieved” — uses the same root as the wrath provoked by Vashti in Esther 1:12; the tension opened in chapter 1 is resolved here. The gallows as self-executed judgment enacts the proverb pattern of Proverbs 26:27.
